parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected clause to read or modify records, then pivot to account takeover or database destruction.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can alter or stack SQL statements, risking data theft, tampering, or destructive database actions.
parsed_findings.proof: `sort` is constrained but `dir` can carry arbitrary SQL, so `ORDER BY name asc; DROP TABLE users;--` is possible.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `?sort=name&dir=asc;DROP TABLE users;--` and observe the query text includes attacker-controlled SQL.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `direction` is interpolated into the `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the SSRF to reach internal admin or metadata endpoints, then pivot to higher-impact data exposure.
parsed_findings.impact: This can expose internal services, metadata, and other sensitive network-only resources through SSRF.
parsed_findings.proof: `req.query.url` is passed directly to `axios.get(u)` and the only filter is a malformed check on line 3, so arbitrary destinations are reachable.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:PORT/` or a cloud metadata URL and the server will fetch and return the response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint is an unauthenticated server-side fetch that lets attackers proxy arbitrary URLs, including internal targets.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use traversal to exfiltrate secrets, then pivot to credentials or internal service access.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read sensitive files accessible to the process, including configs, keys, and source.
parsed_findings.proof: `../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolves outside the base dir.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the server attempt to read a parent-directory file.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The filter removes only one "../", so a crafted path still escapes `/srv/data` and reads arbitrary files.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use cookie injection to reach the deserialization sink, then trigger gadget execution during `resume()`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may gain remote code execution or arbitrary object execution when the endpoint processes the cookie.
parsed_findings.proof: `state` comes from a client cookie and is passed straight into `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a crafted `state` cookie containing a malicious pickle payload and hit `/resume`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, which can execute attacker-controlled code.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the endpoint to run arbitrary commands and then read or alter backup data.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote command execution as the app user.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON `{"name":"x;id#","src":"/var/data/app"}` and observe shell execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `name` is interpolated into a shell command, so a crafted backup name can execute arbitrary commands.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Abuse command execution to pivot into file theft or persistence.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote command execution as the app user.
parsed_findings.proof: `src='/var/data/app;id#'` still passes validation and appends `;id` to the shell command.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON `{"src":"/var/data/app;id#"}` and confirm the injected command runs.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `startswith` check does not stop shell metacharacters in `src`, so path input can break out of `tar`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject payload via `POST /comments`, then trigger execution by visiting the comment list page that renders `html`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can run script in other users' browsers when they view comments.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown(body)` can emit attacker-controlled HTML, and `db.insert_comment({'html': rendered, ...})` persists it for `/comments`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST a comment containing `<script>alert(1)</script>` or unsafe markdown HTML, then fetch `/comments` and render the stored HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Markdown output is stored and later returned without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if raw HTML or unsafe markdown is allowed.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race the check at line 6 with simultaneous writes at lines 8-9.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can double-spend funds and drive the source balance below zero or transfer more than allowed.
parsed_findings.proof: Both requests can read the same `src` at line 5 before either write at lines 8-9 commits.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two parallel POSTs from the same user with the same amount that leaves a small positive balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Balance updates are not atomic, so two concurrent transfers can both pass the check and overspend the same account.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge token -> trigger verify failure -> hit production fallback -> use decoded claims as authenticated identity.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can impersonate users or escalate privileges by choosing token claims.
parsed_findings.proof: Any invalid or unsigned JWT returns decoded claims in production instead of being rejected.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a forged JWT with arbitrary payload and a bad signature; `jwt.verify()` throws, then `jwt.decode()` accepts it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to `jwt.decode()` after signature verification fails, letting attacker-supplied tokens bypass auth.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use timing to recover the valid HMAC, then send a forged body that passes line 6 and reaches line 7.
parsed_findings.impact: This can reduce the search space for the webhook MAC and enable forged event delivery.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can measure response timing while guessing `X-Sig` to distinguish partial matches.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests with near-miss signatures and compare latency distributions against random guesses.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Signature comparison uses `==`, which can leak HMAC validity via timing differences.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the open redirect as a trusted hop in phishing or auth flow hijacking.
parsed_findings.impact: Phishing, token theft via redirect chaining, and trust abuse from a seemingly internal link.
parsed_findings.proof: `startswith('/')` accepts scheme-relative URLs like `//attacker.tld` and Flask will emit them in `Location`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/go?next=//attacker.tld` and observe a redirect to the attacker origin.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `nxt` allows `//evil.com`, so `redirect(nxt)` can send users to an attacker site.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use XXE to exfiltrate sensitive files or pivot to internal HTTP services.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read local files or trigger SSRF through the import endpoint.
parsed_findings.proof: `resolve_entities=True` with `no_network=False` lets attacker-controlled XML resolve external entities during `fromstring()`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject query operators in `req.body.email` or `req.body.password`, then exploit the returned user object to reach the authenticated session path.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bypass login and access arbitrary accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: If the DB driver interprets objects, `email={$ne:null}` and a matching password can make `findOne(q)` return an unintended user.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON with `email` and `password` as operator objects instead of strings, then observe `findOne` matching a record without valid credentials.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted body fields are passed straight into the database lookup, enabling NoSQL operator injection and auth bypass.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF to access internal HTTP admin panels or cloud metadata, then pivot to higher-value credentials or services.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can probe internal services, reach metadata endpoints, or exfiltrate responses through the size side channel.
parsed_findings.proof: `requests.get(url)` issues the outbound request using user input, and `startswith('http')` does not block localhost, RFC1918, or redirect tricks.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:..."}` or a cloud metadata URL to make the server request internal resources.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint fetches an attacker-controlled URL server-side, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata services.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow the stack buffer through `gets`, then hijack control flow if protections are weak.
parsed_findings.impact: This can crash the program and may allow code execution via stack corruption.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets` reads an unbounded line into `buf[64]`, so a long input can overwrite adjacent stack data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and paste more than 63 non-NUL bytes before Enter.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` is inherently unsafe and can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the recovered key to decrypt stored ciphertexts or craft valid ciphertexts for chosen plaintexts.
parsed_findings.impact: Exposure of the key breaks confidentiality for all data encrypted by this helper.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY = b'0123456789abcdef'` is a fixed secret embedded in the file.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read the source, extract the key, then use it to decrypt any `iv + ciphertext` output from `enc()`
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The encryption key is hardcoded in source, so anyone with code access can decrypt or forge ciphertexts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0`, then the bad guard skips validation and line 5 faults.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can trigger a crash and deny service wherever this helper is reachable.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` only rejects non-null `out`, then line 5 writes through `out`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("a", NULL, 4)` and the function reaches `memcpy(out, in, n)` with a null destination.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The null-check is inverted, so a null `out` slips through and `memcpy` dereferences it.

results.stdout_preview: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"pwn","first_file":"01-environment-baseline.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"The null-check is inverted, so a null `out` slips through and `memcpy` dereferences it.","proof":"`if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;`
